Content: Eye injuries are common in blast and fragmentation incidents. Types: corneal abrasion (foreign body in eye — do not rub, irrigate with clean water, patch both eyes as eyes move together), penetrating eye injury (do not remove object, cover with rigid shield — NOT pressure bandage, evacuate urgently), flash blindness (temporary from explosions or laser — reassure, will recover in minutes to hours), chemical splash (irrigate immediately with large volumes of water for minimum 20 minutes). The golden rule for any serious eye injury: cover, do not compress, evacuate. Vision loss is permanent if penetrating injury is mishandled.

Fractures in field conditions must be stabilised before evacuation. Open fracture (bone visible): cover wound with clean dressing, do not push bone back, immobilise, give antibiotics if available (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate), evacuate urgently — infection risk is high. Closed fracture: immobilise joint above and below fracture using improvised splint (rifle, straight branch, rolled sleeping mat) padded with clothing. Check circulation, sensation, and movement (CSM) before and after splinting — pulse distal to fracture must be present. Femur fracture can cause 1-2 litres of internal blood loss — treat for shock.

Suspect spinal injury in: fall from height, vehicle rollover, blast injury, diving accident, any trauma with neck or back pain, or unconscious trauma patient. Do NOT bend, twist, or flex the spine. Log roll technique: minimum 3 people, one controls the head maintaining inline stabilisation, two roll the body as a unit. Improvised cervical collar: rolled sleeping mat or clothing around neck. Improvised stretcher: two rifles through sleeves of two jackets. Patient must be moved on a firm surface. Neurological signs: tingling, weakness, or paralysis in limbs after trauma — treat as spinal injury until proven otherwise.

Common medications carried at section or platoon level in Indian Army. Analgesics: Paracetamol 500mg (pain, fever — safe), Ibuprofen 400mg (pain, inflammation — avoid in dehydration/kidney disease), Morphine auto-injector (severe pain, trauma — causes respiratory depression, never give to head injury). Antibiotics: Amoxicillin (infections), Ciprofloxacin (diarrhoea, wound infection). Anti-malarials: Chloroquine or Primaquine (area-dependent). Anti-diarrhoeal: ORS (rehydration — always preferred over stopping diarrhoea with Loperamide in infection). Antacid: Omeprazole. All medications must be checked for allergy before administration.

IV fluids are administered in haemorrhagic shock when oral fluids are not possible. Types: Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl — replaces blood volume), Ringer's Lactate (preferred in trauma, more physiologically balanced), Dextrose 5% (for hypoglycaemia — not for fluid resuscitation). Permissive hypotension: in penetrating trauma, target systolic BP of 80-90 mmHg rather than normalising BP — prevents clot disruption. Signs of shock: rapid weak pulse, pale cold clammy skin, altered consciousness, reduced urine output. Two large-bore IV lines in antecubital fossa. Intraosseous (IO) route if IV access fails — drill into tibia.

Dengue is transmitted by Aedes mosquito (daytime biter unlike malaria mosquito). Symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, pain behind eyes, bone and joint pain (breakbone fever), rash (red with white islands), bleeding from gums or nose. Warning signs of severe dengue: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, rapid breathing, fatigue. No specific antiviral — treatment is supportive: paracetamol (NOT aspirin or ibuprofen — increases bleeding risk), oral rehydration, rest. Platelet monitoring critical. Evacuate if warning signs present. Prevention: DEET repellent effective against Aedes, remove stagnant water from camp area.

Leptospirosis is caused by Leptospira bacteria found in water contaminated by animal urine (rats, dogs, cattle). Common in flooding, waterlogged terrain after monsoon, and jungle operations. Entry: through cuts in skin or mucous membranes. Symptoms: sudden fever, severe muscle pain (especially calves), headache, red eyes (conjunctival suffusion), jaundice (yellow skin/eyes) in severe cases. Weil's disease (severe form): kidney failure, liver failure, bleeding. Prevention: waterproof boots, avoid wading in floodwater with open wounds, doxycycline prophylaxis in high-risk situations. Treatment: Doxycycline or Penicillin.

Cholera causes profuse rice-water diarrhoea and can kill through dehydration within hours. Caused by Vibrio cholerae in contaminated water. Field sanitation breakdown is the primary cause of outbreaks. ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) is life-saving — 1 litre water, 6 teaspoons sugar, half teaspoon salt. Zinc supplements reduce severity in children. Antibiotic (Doxycycline or Azithromycin) reduces duration. Prevention: only drink purified water, cook food thoroughly, wash hands after defecation and before eating. Camp sanitation: latrines minimum 30m downwind and downhill from water source and 30m from cookhouse.

An Observation Post (OP) provides continuous surveillance of a designated area. OP routine: observer and sentry rotate every 30-60 minutes to prevent fatigue-induced errors. Systematic observation: use binoculars, scan from ground to horizon, near to far, left to right in overlapping arcs. Recording: maintain OP log with time, grid, description, and direction of all activity. Reporting: use SALUTE format — Size, Activity, Location, Uniform/Unit, Time, Equipment. Camouflage of OP: no movement during daylight in exposed positions, no smoking, no shine, no noise. Communication: scheduled radio checks minimum every 2 hours.

Topic: Use of Ground and Dead Ground
Content: Dead ground is terrain not visible from a specific viewpoint — it can conceal friendly or enemy movement. Identifying dead ground: look at the ground between you and a distant point — any hollow, reverse slope, or depression is potential dead ground. Exploitation: use dead ground to approach enemy positions unobserved. Counter-dead-ground: position OPs and sentry posts to cover likely dead ground. Re-entrants (valleys running toward enemy positions) are classic dead ground used for infiltration. In Kargil, Pakistani infiltrators used dead ground extensively to occupy heights undetected by Indian positions.

Tracking is following a person or animal through terrain by reading signs. Track types: footprints (note tread pattern, depth indicating load carried, stride length indicating pace), crushed vegetation, broken branches, disturbed soil, blood spoor. Age of tracks: fresh tracks have sharp edges, moist soil, no insect activity within the track; older tracks have weathered edges, dried appearance. Gait analysis: normal walk shows alternating left-right prints; running shows longer stride and toe-dig; injured person may drag a foot. Counter-tracking: walk on hard surfaces, step on stones, backtrack then change direction, walk in water.

A hide is a concealed position for long-duration observation or sniping. Construction principles: site must have clear observation of target area, covered approach route, concealed exit route (different from entry). Types: scrape (quick, shallow depression), shell scrape (deeper, provides ballistic protection), deep hide (for multi-day occupation — includes overhead cover, sleeping area, waste management). Waste management in hide: all food waste, human waste must be bagged and removed — smell reveals position. Entry and exit: only in darkness, same approach route used to avoid creating visible track. Maintain routine to avoid silhouette against light.

Night navigation is significantly more challenging than day navigation. Techniques: pace counting (know how many paces equal 100m for you — typically 62-66 double paces on flat ground), use of stars (Polaris/North Star is a fixed reference), handrails (linear features — roads, rivers, ridgelines — followed to a destination), attack points (obvious features near objective used as final navigation reference). Reduced speed: night navigation should be planned at 1/3 daytime speed. Red light torches preserve night vision (takes 20-30 minutes for eyes to fully adapt to darkness). NVDs impair depth perception — move slowly and deliberately.

River crossings are dangerous — more soldiers historically have died crossing rivers than in many battles. Assessment: check current speed (throw a stick — fast current = dangerous), depth (probe with pole), banks (steep banks indicate fast deep water), crossing point (widest = shallowest and slowest). Techniques: individual crossing — face upstream, lean on pole, move at 45° downstream angle, unclip pack straps and loosen boots before crossing. Group crossing: line abreast facing upstream, hands on each other's shoulders for stability. Never cross in darkness unless operationally essential. If swept away: float on back, feet downstream to fend off rocks.

Indian Army uses several grenade types. HE Fragmentation (36M): delay fuze 4-5 seconds, lethal radius 10m, danger area 100m, used against personnel in the open and in buildings. Smoke (L83): used for concealment and signalling. White Phosphorus (WP): incendiary and smoke, causes severe burns, burns underwater, used to clear bunkers and create smoke screens. Throwing technique: straighten pin (do not pull with finger — use thumb), firm grip, throw like a cricket ball, take cover immediately. Never cook a grenade (hold it before throwing to reduce reaction time — training accidents result). In confined spaces: stairwells and corridors create lethal overpressure.

The Under Barrel Grenade Launcher (UBGL) is attached to the INSAS or AK-203 rifle, firing 40mm grenades. Effective range: 150-300m. Minimum safe range: 30m (grenades arm after a certain spin count — will not explode if fired too close). Types of 40mm rounds: HE (fragmentation), smoke, illumination, rubber baton (riot control). The UBGL gives a rifle section indirect fire capability at section level. Loading: break open launcher, insert round, close — the trigger is separate from the rifle trigger. Point of aim: use the ladder sight, adjust for range. Clearance: treat a misfired round with extreme caution — wait 30 seconds, remove carefully.

The Nag is India's indigenous third-generation fire-and-forget Anti-Tank Guided Missile. Guidance: imaging infrared (IIR) seeker — locks onto heat signature of tank before launch and tracks autonomously (true fire and forget). Warhead: tandem HEAT — defeats ERA (Explosive Reactive Armour). Variants: Nag (ground launched from NAMICA vehicle), Helina/Dhruvastra (helicopter launched from HAL Rudra and LCH). Range: ground variant 0.5-4km, helicopter variant 7-10km. The missile can attack tank top armour (most vulnerable) in top-attack mode. Successfully completed development trials; induction into Indian Army ongoing.

The Spike ATGM is an Israeli missile system procured by India for infantry anti-armour capability. Variants: Spike-SR (short range, 800m), Spike-MR (medium range, 2.5km), Spike-LR (long range, 4km), Spike-ER (extended range, 8km). Guidance: electro-optical/infrared — man in the loop guidance allows operator to redirect missile in flight and abort if civilians detected. Tandem HEAT warhead defeats all known tank armour. Can be fired from shoulder, vehicle, or helicopter. India procured Spike-MR for infantry units and Spike-LR for helicopters. Advantage over older wire-guided systems: fire and steer, not fire and forget — greater targeting flexibility.

Man-Portable Air Defence Systems (MANPADS) provide infantry units with protection against low-flying aircraft and helicopters. Indian Army uses Igla-S (Russian) and Mistral (French). Igla-S: infrared heat-seeking, range 6km, altitude up to 3.5km, all-aspect engagement capability. Mistral: radar proximity fuze, range 6km, high agility for engaging fast jets. Employment: minimum two launchers per engagement (to counter evasive action), predict aircraft position (lead the target), verify identification before firing (IFF — Identification Friend or Foe is critical). Avoid using near own aircraft corridors. Backblast: minimum 25m clearance behind launcher.

The Bofors FH77B 155mm howitzer is one of the most effective artillery pieces in Indian Army service. Range: 30km with extended range ammunition, 40km+ with Excalibur GPS-guided rounds. Rate of fire: burst 3 rounds in 8 seconds, sustained 2-3 rpm. Crew: 8-10 soldiers. Accuracy: the Bofors was decisive in the 1999 Kargil War — its ability to fire at high angles allowed it to engage Pakistani positions on reverse slopes of very high peaks. Counter-battery fire: the Bofors' survivability comes from rapid displacement after firing (shoot and scoot). Ammunition types: HE, smoke, illumination, Cargo (sub-munitions), Excalibur precision.

Pinaka is India's indigenous multi-barrel rocket system (MBRS), equivalent to the Russian BM-21 Grad or US MLRS. A battery of 6 Pinaka launchers can fire 72 rockets in 44 seconds. Mark I: range 37.5km. Mark II: range 65km. Mark III (guided): range 75km+ with GPS/INS guidance. Warhead types: HE, incendiary, anti-tank sub-munitions, mine-laying. Pinaka is highly mobile — mounted on Tatra truck. Used for area saturation, counter-battery, and deep interdiction missions. Inducted into Indian Army and operated by artillery regiments. Exported to Armenia (used in Nagorno-Karabakh conflict).

Combat engineers (Sappers in Indian Army — Corps of Engineers) support infantry with: mobility (breaching obstacles, building bridges), counter-mobility (minefield laying, demolitions), survivability (field fortifications, bunker construction). Bangalore torpedo: metal tube filled with explosive, used to breach wire obstacles and clear mine lanes — sections pushed together and detonated. Demolitions: C4 plastic explosive — 1.5x more powerful than TNT by weight, shaped charges for cutting steel, cratering charges for denying roads. Mine marking: standard mine markers must be clearly visible from standing height. Never remove markers.

A well-constructed field fortification can multiply the fighting power of a small unit. Shell scrape: individual protection from direct fire and shell fragments, dug quickly (30-45 minutes for two soldiers). Fire trench: deeper than shell scrape, allows fighting while remaining protected, headcover added. Sandbag construction: each layer offset by half a bag from layer below (stretcher bond), minimum 4 layers for protection from small arms, 8 layers for mortar fragments. Overhead cover requires minimum 30cm compacted earth over timber supports to stop mortar fragments. Drainage: all trenches must drain or they fill with rainwater during monsoon.

Anti-tank and anti-personnel minefields must be breached for attacking forces to pass through. Methods: manual (prodding with bayonet at 30° angle every 10cm — slow but reliable), mechanical (mine roller or flail attachment on vehicles), explosive (Bangalore torpedo or line charge). Minefield recording: record mine type, location (grid and distance from markers), density, and fuze type. Gaps in a minefield must be minimum 8m wide for vehicles, 2m wide for infantry. Mark breached lanes clearly with tape or pickets. When in doubt — probe. A missed mine can kill an entire section. Mine-awareness training is mandatory.

Electronic Warfare (EW) involves three elements: Electronic Surveillance (ES — listening to enemy signals), Electronic Attack (EA — jamming enemy communications), Electronic Protection (EP — protecting own signals). Signs of enemy jamming: sudden increase in noise/static on radio, unable to communicate on usual frequency. Counter-jamming: change frequency (frequency hopping radios do this automatically), increase power, reposition antenna, use alternate communication means (messenger, signal panels). EMCON (Emission Control): strict radio silence to deny enemy signals intelligence. Mobile phones emit radio signals continuously — a significant SIGINT vulnerability even when not making calls.

Field telephone systems provide secure communication without the radio emission vulnerability. D-10 field cable can be run up to 5km per reel. Wire laying: run along one side of the road, mark clearly to prevent vehicle damage, cross roads underground if possible. Telephone exchange (field): connects multiple lines. Code: all positions and persons referred to by codename — never use real names or locations on field telephone. Advantages over radio: cannot be intercepted by direction-finding equipment, no frequency allocation needed, no battery-critical radio required. Disadvantage: wire can be cut by enemy, takes time to lay.

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are now common in modern warfare including the India-Pakistan and India-China contexts. Indian Army uses: Heron (strategic surveillance), Searcher Mk II (tactical surveillance), Rustom-2 (MALE UAV under development), small quadcopters for section-level reconnaissance. Enemy drone threats: quadcopters for surveillance, fixed-wing for strike. Signs of enemy drone: buzzing sound (quadcopter), thermal signature on NVDs, visual sighting. Counter-drone measures: electronic jamming (disrupts GPS control link), kinetic (shooting — requires permission), net guns (for small drones). Always report unidentified aerial objects. Avoid using mobile phones or active radios when drone is suspected overhead.

Cyber threats to soldiers in the field include: social engineering (fake contacts on social media gathering operational information), phishing (fake emails/messages with malicious links), SIM cloning (mobile number duplicated to intercept calls). Operational security on social media: never post unit movements, troop positions, equipment details, or deployment timings. Even seemingly harmless photos can reveal location through metadata and background details. Password security: strong passwords (12+ characters, mix of types), never share passwords, change regularly. Official communication only through secured government networks. Report suspicious digital activity to the unit signals officer immediately.

The Para Special Forces (Para SF) are India's elite special operations forces, drawn from the Parachute Regiment. Selection: gruelling probation course with very high attrition rate — candidates must complete a long-distance solo navigation exercise with minimal rations. Roles: direct action, special reconnaissance, hostage rescue, counter-terrorism, foreign internal defence. Units: 1 Para SF, 2 Para SF, 3 Para SF, 4 Para SF, 9 Para SF, 10 Para SF, 21 Para SF. Para SF conducted Operation Sunrise cross-border strikes in Myanmar (2015) and surgical strikes across LOC in Pakistan (2016). Equipped with: Glock 17, M4 carbine, SIG716, various specialist weapons.

MARCOS (Marine Commandos) are the Indian Navy's special operations force, considered among India's most elite units. Selection process lasts 3 years. Roles: maritime counter-terrorism, underwater demolition, ship seizure, beach reconnaissance, anti-piracy. Training: HALO/HAHO parachuting, combat diving, close quarter battle, VBSS (Visit Board Search and Seizure). MARCOS were deployed in the 2008 Mumbai attacks (26/11) — cleared terrorists from the boat MV Kuber and assisted in other operations. Equipped with: Tavor TAR-21, MP5, HK33, underwater weapons, various diving equipment. The MARCOS motto is 'The Few, The Fearless'.

The Garud Commando Force is the Indian Air Force's special operations unit. Primary roles: airfield seizure and defence, CSAR (Combat Search and Rescue) of downed pilots, counter-terrorism at air force bases, and support to other special operations. Selection is highly competitive, drawing from existing IAF personnel. Training includes airborne operations, combat diving, close quarter battle, SERE (Survival, Evasion, Resistance, Escape). Garud units are permanently positioned at key IAF bases for airfield defence. During the 2019 Balakot air strikes, Garud units were on standby for CSAR operations in case any IAF pilot was downed in Pakistani territory.

Logistics in the Indian Army follows the principle: 'Amateurs talk strategy, professionals talk logistics.' Supply classes: Class I (food and water), Class II (clothing and equipment), Class III (fuel and lubricants), Class IV (construction materials), Class V (ammunition), Class VIII (medical supplies). Ammunition resupply: units maintain a basic load (day of fire) and are resupplied based on consumption. High-altitude resupply: Siachen posts receive supplies primarily by helicopter (Cheetah and ALH Dhruv) as road access is impossible. Air maintenance (air dropping) used when helicopter landing is not possible. Water forward to troops in desert and high altitude is a critical logistics task.

In forward areas, soldiers may need to cook their own food using field cooking sets. Importance of hot meals for morale — Unit commanders are responsible for ensuring troops receive hot food when operationally possible. Water resupply: reverse osmosis water purification units (ROWPU) can purify 1,500 litres per hour from any water source — unit level capability. Jerrycan discipline: fuel and water jerrycans are different colours — never mix them. Forward water points: established as close to troops as possible. In desert operations (Rajasthan), water discipline is mission-critical — soldiers must carry minimum 4 litres. Camel-back hydration systems increasingly issued.

Calling for artillery fire is a critical skill. Fire mission format: Warning order ('Fire mission'), Target location (grid 6-8 digit), Target description, Method of engagement, Method of fire and control. Observer adjustments: the observer sees where the first round lands and adjusts: ADD (increase range), DROP (decrease range), LEFT/RIGHT (in metres), UP (elevation). Danger close: when friendly troops are within 600m of target (artillery) — must be declared explicitly. Lifting fire: 'LIFT' when friendly troops are about to assault through target area. Shifts: 'SHIFT, direction, distance'. Key principle: artillery kills through fragmentation — soldiers must be in cover during friendly artillery.

Close Air Support (CAS) is air-delivered fire in close proximity to friendly forces. Requesting CAS: pilot must know your position before target location (to avoid fratricide). 9-Line CAS brief: IP (Initial Point), Heading to target, Distance, Target elevation, Target description, Target location, Type mark (smoke, laser, IR strobe), Location of friendlies, Egress direction. Laser designation: laser target designator marks target for laser-guided bombs. Fratricide prevention: VS-17 signal panels, IR strobes (visible only on NVDs), coloured smoke. Minimum safe distance for CAS: depends on aircraft type and munition — coordinate with the pilot. Mark your position before calling CAS.

Hostage rescue is one of the most dangerous military tasks. Principles: speed, surprise, and violence of action — hesitation costs lives. Phases: intelligence gathering, planning, rehearsal, insertion, assault, extraction. Assault: simultaneous entry through multiple points to prevent hostages being used as shields, achieve dominance in under 30 seconds. Shoot/no-shoot: positive identification of target before firing — hostages and terrorists will be mixed together in the dark and chaos. Flashbang (stun grenade) disorients occupants without lethal effect — 2-3 seconds of disorientation window. After assault: immediate medical aid to all casualties, account for all hostages.

The National Security Guard (NSG) is India's premier federal counter-terrorism force, under Ministry of Home Affairs. Nicknamed 'Black Cats' for their black uniforms. Two elements: Special Action Group (SAG — military personnel on secondment) and Special Ranger Group (SRG — police personnel). Role: counter-terrorism, hostage rescue, VIP protection, IED disposal, aircraft hijacking response. NSG conducted Operation Black Tornado during 26/11 Mumbai attacks (2008) — clearing the Taj Hotel, Trident Hotel, and Nariman House. HQ: Manesar, Haryana, with hubs in Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, and Hyderabad. NSG also manages the National Bomb Data Centre.

The HAL Tejas is India's indigenously developed light combat aircraft (LCA), inducted into the Indian Air Force in 2016. Engine: GE F404 (Mark 1), GE F414 (Mark 2 — more powerful). Speed: Mach 1.8. Weapons: R-73 AAM (dogfight), Derby/Astra BVR AAMs, bombs, rockets, gun (23mm twin-barrel). Fly-by-wire flight control system makes it inherently unstable for better agility. Mark 2 will have AESA radar, more powerful engine, air-to-air refuelling capability, and carrier suitability. The Navy variant (Tejas NMR) is under development for INS Vikrant. India is the 6th country to develop a supersonic fly-by-wire combat aircraft indigenously.

The HAL Advanced Light Helicopter (ALH) Dhruv is a twin-engine multi-role helicopter developed and manufactured indigenously by HAL. Variants: Dhruv (utility/transport), ALH-WSI Rudra (armed — guns, rockets, Helina missiles), LCH (Light Combat Helicopter — dedicated attack). Engine: Shakti (indigenous development of Turbomeca TM333). Speed: 290 km/h. Range: 350km. Ceiling: 6,000m (critical for high-altitude operations in Ladakh and Siachen). Dhruv operates in extreme conditions — has operated at altitudes exceeding 8,000m. Equipped with: EFIS glass cockpit, NVD-compatible lighting, flotation gear for maritime variant. Used extensively for CASEVAC in high-altitude postings.

Indian Air Force operates several fast jet types. Su-30 MKI (primary air superiority fighter): twin-engine, supermaneuverable, range 3,000km, carries BrahMos-A air-launched cruise missile. Mirage 2000 (precision strike): laser-guided bombs, SCALP cruise missile — used in Balakot 2019 airstrikes. MiG-29 (fleet air defence): phased out and upgraded versions with Indian Navy (MiG-29K from INS Vikramaditya). Rafale (inducted 2020): Meteor BVR missile (range 100km+), SCALP cruise missile, SPECTRA electronic warfare suite — currently most capable IAF fighter. Jaguar (ground attack): being phased out. F-21 and MQ-9B under consideration for future induction.

INS Vikrant (IAC-1) is India's first indigenously built aircraft carrier, commissioned in September 2022. Displacement: 45,000 tonnes (full load). Length: 262m. Aircraft capacity: up to 30 aircraft (MiG-29K fighters, Ka-31 AEW helicopters, MH-60R ASW helicopters, ALH Dhruv). STOBAR (Short Take-Off But Arrested Recovery) configuration — ski-jump for takeoff, arrestor wires for landing. Propulsion: 4 General Electric LM2500 gas turbines, 88MW total power, speed 28 knots. Manufactured at Cochin Shipyard. Steel hull made from Indian-produced DMR 249A special grade steel. Represents a major milestone in India's indigenous defence manufacturing.

Indian Navy operates conventional and nuclear submarines. Conventional (diesel-electric): Kalvari class (6 Scorpene-type — French design, indigenous build at MDL Mumbai), Shishumar class (German Type 209, aging). Nuclear: INS Arihant (SSBN — ballistic missile submarine, carries K-15 SLBMs), INS Arighat (second SSBN commissioned 2024), INS Chakra (nuclear attack submarine — leased from Russia). Submarine stealth: extremely quiet, difficult to detect. Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) against them: P-8I Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft, helicopter sonobuoys, ship-mounted sonar, torpedoes. Future: 6 additional conventional submarines under P-75I project.

The 1971 war resulted in the creation of Bangladesh from East Pakistan. Indian Army launched a swift 13-day campaign (December 3-16, 1971) coordinated across four fronts. Key operations: capture of Dhaka, amphibious landing at Cox's Bazar, airborne assault by 2 Para Battalion at Tangail (one of India's few combat airborne operations). Pakistan's Eastern Command surrendered on December 16 — the largest military surrender since World War II (93,000 POWs). Indian Navy blockaded East Pakistan, INS Vikrant ensured no escape by sea. Chief of Army Staff: General S.H.F.J. Manekshaw (later promoted to Field Marshal). The war is studied as a model of joint operations.

The 1962 Sino-Indian War was a significant military defeat that shaped Indian Army doctrine. Background: border dispute over McMahon Line (Arunachal Pradesh) and Aksai Chin. Key battles: Namka Chu, Rezang La (Major Shaitan Singh's Charlie Company — 114 soldiers held against thousands of Chinese, all killed, posthumous PVC to Shaitan Singh), Se La Pass. Lessons learned: inadequate logistics and supply to forward posts, political interference in military decisions, poor equipment (World War II era), lack of high-altitude warfare training. Post-1962 reforms: formation of high-altitude warfare units, significant investment in border infrastructure (now being accelerated under BRO), Corps of Mountain Artillery established.

Operation Parakram was the largest military mobilisation in India since 1971, triggered by the terrorist attack on the Indian Parliament (December 13, 2001, carried out by Lashkar-e-Taiba and Jaish-e-Mohammed). India mobilised approximately 500,000 troops to the Pakistan border. The standoff lasted approximately 10 months without leading to open war. Lessons from the mobilisation: the time taken to mobilise (weeks rather than days) revealed need for a Cold Start / Pro-Active Operations doctrine allowing faster mobilisation of smaller strike formations. The operation also revealed gaps in equipment, especially tank ammunition stocks, mine-clearing capability, and medical infrastructure along the Pakistan border.

India's military gallantry awards (in order of precedence): Param Vir Chakra (PVC) — highest, awarded for most conspicuous bravery in presence of enemy, only 21 awarded since independence (all but one posthumously). Maha Vir Chakra (MVC) — second highest, conspicuous gallantry in the presence of the enemy. Vir Chakra (VrC) — third, acts of bravery in the presence of the enemy. Ashok Chakra — highest peacetime gallantry (equivalent to PVC), awarded for gallantry not in the presence of the enemy. Kirti Chakra and Shaurya Chakra follow. Sena Medal, Nau Sena Medal, Vayu Sena Medal — service-specific for lesser acts of gallantry. Vishist Seva Medal for distinguished service.

The Indian Army maintains a regimental system inherited from the British, with strong regimental identity and traditions. Major infantry regiments: Brigade of Guards, Punjab Regiment, Grenadiers, Maratha Light Infantry, Rajput Regiment, Sikh Regiment, Sikh Light Infantry, Dogra Regiment, Garhwal Rifles, Kumaon Regiment, Assam Regiment, Bihar Regiment, Mahar Regiment, JAT Regiment, Rajputana Rifles, Naga Regiment, Ladakh Scouts. Each regiment has distinct traditions, cap badges, and recruiting areas. The regimental system builds fierce unit loyalty (izzat) which motivates soldiers in battle. Gurkha Rifles (5 regiments remaining with Indian Army) maintain Nepalese recruitment.

Topic: Corps of Engineers and Support Arms
Content: Indian Army support arms and services: Corps of Engineers (Sappers and Miners — motto 'Sarvatra' meaning Everywhere), Corps of Signals, Army Aviation Corps, Army Air Defence, Army Service Corps (logistics), Army Medical Corps, Army Ordnance Corps, Army Education Corps, Judge Advocate General's Branch, Remount and Veterinary Corps (horses and mules still used in high altitude), Military Nursing Service, Corps of Military Police. Armoured Corps (tanks and mechanised forces). Artillery (Regiment of Artillery). Intelligence Corps. Each arm and service has distinct training centres: Engineers at Pune, Signals at Jabalpur, Artillery at Nasik Road/Devlali.

Pay structure under 7th Pay Commission (implemented 2016). Pay levels for Other Ranks: Sepoy Level 3 (₹21,700 basic), Naik Level 4 (₹25,500), Havildar Level 5 (₹29,200), Naib Subedar Level 6 (₹35,400), Subedar Level 7 (₹44,900), Subedar Major Level 8 (₹47,600). Allowances: Military Service Pay (MSP — ₹5,200/month for JCOs/ORs), High Altitude Allowance (up to ₹25,000/month for Siachen — Zone V), Field Area Allowance, Hardship Allowance, Free Ration (or Ration Money Allowance), Free Accommodation. Total emoluments significantly exceed basic pay due to allowances. Pay is credited to bank account by 25th of each month.

Types of leave for Indian Army soldiers: Annual Leave (60 days per year for ORs, can be accumulated up to 300 days), Casual Leave (30 days per year — not encashable), Furlough (extended leave for soldiers in certain conditions), Sick Leave (as certified by medical officer — does not count against leave quota), Special Disability Leave (for injury in service), Maternity/Paternity Leave. Leave must be sanctioned by the Commanding Officer. Leave during operational deployments is subject to operational requirements and may be curtailed. Soldiers must leave emergency contact details before proceeding on leave. Emergency leave (Telegraph leave) can be granted in family emergencies.

Indian Army provides accommodation through: Cantonments (permanent military areas managed by Cantonment Boards — 62 in India), Military Stations (military-controlled residential areas outside cantonments), and Government Accommodation (various types: Type A to Type G based on rank). Housing allocation: entitlement based on rank, family status, and posting. Married accommodation is a major concern — waiting lists can be long at popular stations. Married Accommodation Project (MAP): ongoing project to construct additional housing. Hiring of private accommodation when government quarters unavailable: House Rent Allowance (HRA) is provided. Rules for accommodation during TDY/courses: transit accommodation in Officers/JCO/OR Messes.

Visual signals allow communication without radio in noise-restricted or radio-silent situations. Standard Indian Army hand signals: Come here (beckoning motion), Halt (raised fist), Enemy seen (pointing finger), Take cover (pushing hands downward), Move forward (arm swung forward), Move to flank (arm extended to flank), Speed up (circular arm motion), Slow down (arm waved down slowly), Section attack (arm pointed at objective then sweeping motion), Ambush (fist then pointing at ground). VS-17 signal panel: bright orange/pink, used for air-ground identification, ground-to-air communication, marking positions for extraction. Heliograph mirror: sun reflection used for long-distance signalling.

When no radio communication with aircraft is possible, ground signals are used. Standard ground to air symbols (made with rocks, clothing, or trampled vegetation, minimum 10m long): V = require assistance, X = require medical assistance, → = proceeding in this direction, F = require food and water, LL = all is well, N = No, Y = Yes. Smoke signals: orange smoke (friendly position), yellow smoke (landing zone clear), red smoke (emergency). At night: torch signals, fire, or IR strobe (visible to NVD-equipped aircraft). In jungle: cut clearing in canopy as landing zone marker — minimum 30x30m for helicopter. Always position ground signals in open ground visible from altitude.

Fire provides warmth, ability to signal, purify water, cook food, and psychological comfort. Fire triangle: fuel, heat, oxygen — remove any one and fire dies. Preparation: tinder (fine dry material — dry grass, cotton lint, shredded bark), kindling (pencil-thin sticks), fuel (larger logs). Methods: issued military lighter (most reliable — maintain as priority kit), waterproof matches, flint and steel striker (reliable in wet), bow drill (friction method — effective but slow, requires dry wood). Never start fire in hide position — smoke and light compromise concealment. Fire in cold weather: construct raised platform to prevent fire sinking into snow.

In a survival situation, being found is the primary task. Priority of signals: visual (most important — can be seen by aircraft during day), audio (whistle — internationally recognised distress signal is three blasts), electronic (EPIRB, PLB, mobile phone). Rule of threes for signals: three fires in a triangle, three whistle blasts, three shots. Daytime signals: signal mirror (can be seen 50km+), bright colour, smoke (dark smoke visible against light sky — use rubber/plastic, light smoke visible against dark jungle — use green leaves). Night: fire, torch. Always attract attention as soon as the decision to signal is made — do not wait.

Helicopter drills are essential for rapid insertion and extraction. Approach to helicopter: approach from the front and sides NEVER from the rear (tail rotor kills). Move in crouched position under rotor arc. Load by marshaller's direction. Seatbelt must be fastened during flight. Hot refuel: remain seated with seatbelts fastened, no smoking. Fast rope insertion: used when helicopter cannot land, soldier descends rope at controlled speed. Rappelling from helicopter: slower, uses standard rappelling equipment. In helicopter under fire: stay calm, trust the pilot, get low. Crash landing drill: brace position, exit through designated emergency exits immediately.

Indian Army maintains airborne capability through the Parachute Regiment (non-SF battalions) and Para SF. Types of parachuting: Static line (automatic deployment, used for mass drops, suitable for low altitudes from 300m), HAHO (High Altitude High Opening — open at altitude, glide long distance, useful for covert insertion), HALO (High Altitude Low Opening — freefall, open at low altitude, radar cross-section reduced). Aircraft used: IL-76, C-130J Hercules (primary for Para SF — can do low-level covert insertions), C-17 Globemaster. Mass tactical parachute drops last used in combat in 1971 (Tangail). Airborne operations extremely vulnerable during descent — must achieve surprise.

A deliberate river crossing is a complex operation requiring coordination. Phases: reconnaissance (identify crossing sites, enemy dispositions), planning (allocate crossing means, fire support, timings), preparation (bridge equipment, boats assembled), and crossing (assault, build-up, exploitation). Equipment: Bailey bridge (can be assembled without cranes, carries 70-ton MBT), M3 Amphibious Rig (self-propelled pontoon ferry — carries one MBT), inflatable assault boats (infantry). River crossing is extremely vulnerable — maximum speed is essential. In 1971, Indian Army's rapid river crossing of the Meghna using helicopters was decisive in reaching Dhaka.

Military deception (MILDEC) aims to cause the enemy to make incorrect decisions. Techniques: feints (attacking in wrong direction to draw attention), demonstrations (show of force without intent to attack), ruses (misleading information), dummy positions (decoy vehicles and equipment), electronic deception (false radio traffic). In 1971, India used MILDEC effectively — amphibious feints threatened Karachi while the main effort was in the East. Signature management: control what the enemy can detect — limit vehicle movements at predictable times, use blackout discipline, maintain radio silence on main effort direction. Counter-deception: be sceptical of information that confirms your existing plan perfectly.

Biological warfare agents are living organisms or toxins that cause disease. Categories: bacterial (anthrax, plague, cholera), viral (smallpox, viral haemorrhagic fevers), toxins (Ricin, Botulinum toxin). Indicators of biological attack: unusual pattern of illness in unit (multiple soldiers sick simultaneously), dead animals in area, unexplained aerosol sprays, unusual munition types. No immediate symptoms — incubation period of 1-14 days (makes attribution difficult). Protective measures: respirator (NBC mask), vaccination (where available), decontamination. Report any suspected biological event immediately to chain of command and medical officer. Anthrax spores are the most likely weaponised biological agent — can survive for decades in soil.

Dental pain can be incapacitating in the field. Common emergencies: toothache (dental abscess — severe pain, may have swelling of jaw/cheek, fever), broken tooth, lost filling. Temporary measures: oil of cloves (eugenol) applied with cotton pellet to cavity reduces pain, temporary filling materials (Cavit), analgesics (Ibuprofen more effective than Paracetamol for dental pain). Dental abscess: antibiotics (Amoxicillin 500mg TID for 7 days) to control infection, does NOT cure the problem — requires dental treatment. Prevention: brush teeth twice daily even in field, dental fitness is checked at annual medical. Severe jaw swelling from abscess can compromise airway — evacuate.

NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) is one of the most common long-term occupational hazards for soldiers. Causes: weapons fire (especially in enclosed spaces), artillery, aircraft engines, explosives. Prevention: always wear hearing protection during training and operations. Types: disposable foam earplugs (NRR 29dB), electronic hearing protection (allows normal speech while blocking impulse noise — ideal for tactical use), double protection (plugs + earmuffs) required for artillery. NIHL is irreversible — damage accumulates over a career. Tinnitus (ringing in ears) after weapons exposure without protection indicates hearing damage has occurred. Annual hearing tests (audiometry) are mandatory.

Common skin conditions affecting soldiers in the field. Prickly heat (Miliaria): sweat gland blockage in hot humid conditions — small red itchy bumps, treated by cooling, calamine lotion, and talcum powder; prevent by wearing loose cotton clothing. Tinea (Ringworm/Athlete's foot/Jock itch): fungal infection common in hot humid conditions — red scaly itchy patches. Treatment: Clotrimazole cream twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Prevention: dry skin thoroughly after washing, change socks daily. Cellulitis: bacterial skin infection — hot red swollen painful area, may have fever — requires antibiotics, elevate affected limb, monitor closely for spreading. Pressure sores (in immobilised casualties): reposition every 2 hours.

Field sanitation prevents disease — historically more soldiers die from disease than combat. Latrine construction: minimum 30m from water source, downhill and downwind from camp, 1m deep trench (cat scratch latrine for short stops), fly-proof cover, ash/lime after each use, urination point 10m from latrine. Hand washing: mandatory after latrine, before eating — soap and water minimum 20 seconds. Food hygiene: all utensils washed in hot soapy water then rinsed in clean water. Food covered at all times against flies. Dead animal disposal: bury immediately, minimum 1m deep, lime if available. Fly control: flies transmit typhoid, dysentery, cholera.

The standard combat load for an Indian infantryman is designed for sustained operations. Fighting order (minimum for combat): weapon, ammunition (210 rounds INSAS or 90 rounds AK-203 basic load), water (2 litres minimum), personal medical kit, food for 24 hours, radio (section radio operator). Total weight: approximately 15-20 kg. Marching order (full equipment for sustained ops): adds sleeping system, change of clothes, additional rations, full medical kit, shelter components. Total: 35-45 kg. High altitude additions: down sleeping bag, additional clothing layers, snow equipment add 10-15 kg more. Load management is a commander's responsibility — overloaded soldiers cannot fight effectively.

Night Vision Goggles (NVGs) amplify ambient light (moonlight, starlight) to provide tactical vision in darkness. Tube generations: Gen 1 (basic, heavy distortion), Gen 2 (improved, used by Indian Army ORs), Gen 3 (best performance, used by Para SF and special forces). Wearing: mounted on helmet mount, binocular NVGs (depth perception maintained), monocular NVGs (depth perception impaired — move slowly). Critical limitations: reduced field of view (tunnel vision — approximately 40° vs normal 180°), no colour, cannot see through glass, performance degraded in complete darkness without any ambient light. Infrared illuminator extends range but emits IR signature visible to enemy NVDs.

Indian Army uses several tiers of body armour. Soft armour (NIJ Level IIIA): protects against pistol rounds and fragmentation, worn under uniform or as lightweight tactical vest, flexible but no rifle protection. Hard armour (NIJ Level III/IV ceramic plates): front and back plates in plate carrier, stops 7.62mm rifle rounds (Level III), stops armour-piercing rounds (Level IV). Helmet: Kevlar/composite ballistic helmet — protects against fragmentation and pistol rounds (not rifle rounds). Groin protector and neck collar available for high-threat operations. Weight trade-off: full body armour weighs 12-15 kg and significantly degrades mobility — commanders must balance protection vs operational mobility.

Indian Army Doctrine (published by Army Training Command) is based on the concept of 'Ensuring Certain Victory.' Key doctrinal concepts: Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs) — modular combined arms formation for faster mobilisation below division level, replacing the older Corps-level strike corps model. Network-Centric Warfare: integration of sensors, decision-makers, and shooters through digital communications. Two-Front threat: doctrine addresses simultaneous threat from China and Pakistan. Sub-conventional operations (counter-insurgency) doctrine based on lessons from decades of operations in J&K and Northeast. Future doctrine focus: space, cyber, and cognitive domains as new dimensions of warfare.

ROE in counter-insurgency operations in India (especially J&K under AFSPA) is more permissive than peacetime but strictly governed. Right of Private Defence (Section 96-106 IPC): every citizen including soldier has the right to defend themselves and others. Military context: soldiers must identify target as hostile before engaging. Warning before opening fire (where tactically feasible): 'Ruko, Haath Upar Karo' (Stop, Hands Up), then warning shot, then aimed fire. No fire into crowds without specific threat. Collateral damage: civilian casualties must be immediately reported and investigated. ROE cards carried by all soldiers define exact permissions and restrictions for that operation.

Indian Army is modernising towards future warfare. Key programmes: FICV (Future Infantry Combat Vehicle) — replacement for BMP-2, indigenous design competition ongoing. Arjun MK2 — upgraded main battle tank with Trophy active protection system. Integrated Air Command and Control System (IACCS). Battlefield Management System (BMS) — digital situational awareness for infantry. Communication on the Move (CotM). ATAGS (Advanced Towed Artillery Gun System) — 155mm/52 calibre indigenous howitzer, longest range artillery gun (48km). Loitering munitions (kamikaze drones) — SkyStriker procured from Israel. Directed Energy Weapons (lasers) — DRDO developing DURGA (Directionally Unrestricted Ray-Gun Array).

Urinary issues are common in field conditions. Urinary Tract Infection (UTI): burning on urination, frequent urge, cloudy or smelly urine, lower abdominal pain — treat with Ciprofloxacin 500mg twice daily for 7 days, increase fluid intake. Prevention: urinate after long patrols, maintain hygiene, stay hydrated. Urinary retention: inability to pass urine — caused by cold weather or anxiety — warmth and relaxation usually help mild cases, catheterisation for complete retention. Haematuria (blood in urine) after blast injury or abdominal trauma indicates kidney or bladder injury — evacuate urgently.

Abdominal injuries from blast, gunshot, or shrapnel are life-threatening. Signs: abdominal pain and rigidity, distension, guarding, referred shoulder pain, signs of shock. Evisceration (bowel protruding through wound): do NOT push bowel back, cover with moist clean dressing, keep patient warm, do NOT give oral fluids, evacuate urgently. Abdominal gunshot wound: even if patient appears stable — all cases require surgical evaluation. Do not give food or water by mouth (nil by mouth — patient may need anaesthesia). Morphine for pain relief but document its use clearly as it may mask signs of deterioration.

Essential military knots every soldier must master. Figure-8: strong stopper knot for rappelling anchors — does not slip under load. Bowline: loop knot that does not tighten under load — used for rescue (loop around casualty for hauling). Reef knot: joins two ropes of equal thickness — used for bandaging and improvised stretchers. Clove hitch: quick attachment to pole or carabiner, adjustable — used for anchoring to trees. Prussik: friction knot that grips under load but slides when unloaded — used for self-arrest and ascending a fixed rope. All knots must be dressed (neat) and backed up to prevent working loose under vibration.

A successful ambush requires surprise, concentration of fire, and violence of action. Types: linear ambush (along a track), L-shaped ambush (covers kill zone and escape route), T-ambush. Ground selection: straight stretch of track, obstacle to prevent escape, covered withdrawal route. Initiation: command-initiated ensures simultaneous fire — the kill zone must be saturated immediately. Cut-off groups prevent escape. After ambush: assault through kill zone, collect documents and equipment, immediate withdrawal before enemy reaction force arrives — do not stay longer than 15 minutes post-initiation.

The most dangerous moment in an ambush is the first few seconds — response must be instinctive. If in kill zone (receiving direct fire): immediate aggressive return of fire and assault through — hesitation is fatal, moving into the enemy not away reduces exposure time. If in rear element outside kill zone: flank the ambush, attack the ambush party from the side. At halts: sentries out immediately, never halt in obvious ambush sites such as re-entrants or tree lines. Variable patrol speeds, routes, and timings prevent pattern-setting. Immediate Action drills must be rehearsed before every patrol.

Calling for artillery fire is a critical skill at all levels. Fire mission format: Warning order, Target location (6-8 digit grid), Target description, Method of engagement, Method of fire and control. Adjustment: observer adjusts from first round — ADD (increase range), DROP (decrease range), LEFT/RIGHT in metres. Danger close: when friendly troops are within 600m of target for artillery — must be declared explicitly, commander's risk accepted. After adjustment: FIRE FOR EFFECT is the order to fire for maximum effect. Shell types: HE (high explosive), SMOKE, ILLUM (illumination), WP (white phosphorus). Lifting fire: 'LIFT' order given when troops are about to assault through target area.

Close Air Support (CAS) is air-delivered fire in close proximity to friendly forces. Requesting CAS: pilot must know your position before target location to avoid fratricide. 9-Line CAS brief includes: IP (Initial Point), heading to target, distance, target elevation, target description, target location, type mark (smoke or laser), location of friendlies, and egress direction. Fratricide prevention: VS-17 signal panels, IR strobes visible only on NVDs, and coloured smoke all used to mark own positions. Minimum safe distance for CAS depends on aircraft type and munition — always coordinate. Mark your position clearly before any CAS is delivered.

Hostage rescue is one of the most dangerous military tasks. Principles: speed, surprise, and violence of action — hesitation costs lives. Phases: intelligence gathering, planning, rehearsal, insertion, assault, extraction. Assault: simultaneous entry through multiple points to prevent hostages being used as shields. Achieve dominance in under 30 seconds. Stun grenade (flashbang): disorients occupants without lethal effect — 2-3 second window for entry team. After assault: immediate medical aid to all casualties, account for all hostages, secure the objective fully before declaring clear. Debrief within 1 hour — memories are sharpest immediately after.

Topic: Combat Swimming and Water Obstacle Crossing
Content: Soldiers may need to swim water obstacles carrying equipment. Combat swimming: sidestroke (quieter than front crawl), equipment in waterproof bag or slung across back. Improvised buoyancy aids: trouser legs tied at ankles and swung overhead to trap air — can support a person for 10 minutes; rucksack with air sealed inside provides additional buoyancy. Current technique: swim at 45 degrees downstream (quartering), never directly across fast current which causes exhaustion. Cold water: reduces swimming ability and can cause cold shock cardiac arrest — controlled entry is essential. Pre-crossing: check depth with pole, assess current speed by throwing a stick.

The Tata LPTA series (4x4 and 6x6) is the backbone logistics vehicle of the Indian Army. Used for: troop transport, cargo, artillery towing, water tanker, fuel bowser, and ambulance roles. The 6x6 variant can carry 10 tonnes off-road and negotiate difficult mountain tracks critical for Ladakh and Arunachal Pradesh. Cold start procedures in extreme cold: glow plugs pre-heated, appropriate grade oil for temperature. In snow: tyre chains mandatory, low-range 4WD engaged. Self-recovery: winch capability on most variants. Major recovery: tow bar connection to Stallion or Castor recovery vehicles.

Topic: Small Arms Maintenance in Extreme Conditions
Content: Weapons require adapted maintenance in extreme environments. Extreme cold (Siachen, Ladakh): use Arctic-grade CLP — standard oil thickens below -20°C preventing weapon function, remove all excess lubricant from firing pin and bolt carrier, keep weapon inside sleeping bag at night to prevent freezing. Desert (Rajasthan, Kutch): cover muzzle and ejection port with tape when not in action, clean more frequently as sand accelerates wear, avoid spraying oil which attracts and holds sand — use dry lubricant. Jungle (Northeast): prevent rust — wipe down daily with lightly oiled cloth, inspect barrel for moisture-induced corrosion, check magazine lips weekly.

SATCOM allows long-range secure voice and data communication beyond tactical radio range. Indian Army uses INSAT-based satellite communications at strategic level, tactical SATCOM terminals (TSCT) at brigade and division level, and VSAT for static headquarters. Advantages: long range, encrypted, difficult to direction-find. Disadvantages: requires line-of-sight to satellite, setup time of 5-15 minutes, potential for satellite jamming by advanced adversaries. GSAT-7A (Rukmini) is the dedicated military communication satellite for the IAF. The Indian Navy operates GSAT-7. Battery power management critical in field — SATCOM terminals consume significantly more power than tactical radios.

Heliborne operations insert troops rapidly by helicopter to seize objectives. Load plan: heaviest equipment and first elements to deploy go in last. LZ requirements: minimum 50x50m for single helicopter, clear of obstacles, firm ground, marked with panel or smoke. HLS preparation: troops establish 360-degree security before helicopter approaches. Fast rope insertion: used when helicopter cannot land, soldier descends rope at controlled speed — requires practice. In 1971, heliborne crossing of the Meghna river was operationally decisive. HAL Dhruv and Mi-17 V5 are primary Indian Army assault helicopters. Always designate primary and alternate LZs before any heliborne operation.

Jungle warfare fundamentally changes normal tactics. Reduced visibility (10-50m rather than 500m+): close-range firefights dominate, all-round defence mandatory at all times, flanks are always exposed. Key principles: navigate by compass bearing and pace count (no visible landmarks), maintain strict noise discipline (sound travels far in jungle), small unit initiative is essential (large formations cannot be seen or controlled), ambushes are highly effective. Movement speed: 1-2 km/hour maximum in dense jungle. Counter Insurgency and Jungle Warfare School (CIJWS) at Vairengte, Mizoram trains soldiers and officers from India and friendly nations.

Desert warfare offers unique tactical challenges. Visibility: extremely high (20-30km) — forces visible from great distances. Concealment: very difficult — limited to rock formations, wadis, camel scrub. Night operations: essential for movement — thermal imaging gives major advantage to well-equipped forces. Heat management: schedule operations at night in Rajasthan summer, rest in shade during day. Dust: degrades all optics, engines, and weapons — maintenance schedule doubles. Vehicle-dependent: infantry cannot operate independently over long distances in desert terrain. Sand storms (Andhi): can reduce visibility to zero, halt all operations, and cause navigation errors.

Cold weather operations present extreme operational challenges. Movement: snow reduces infantry speed by 50-70%, exhaustion sets in rapidly, skis or snowshoes necessary in deep snow. Weapons: oil thickens and batteries lose 50% power at -20 degrees Celsius, antennas become brittle and break. Shelter: quinzhee snow dome can save lives in emergency — constructed by piling snow, letting it sinter for 2 hours, then hollowing out. Snow blindness: UV reflection causes corneal burns — wear wrap-around snow goggles or improvise by cutting narrow slits in dark card. Frostbite management: rewarm only if no risk of refreezing — never rub frozen tissue.

Information Operations (IO) affect the information environment of the adversary. Enemy psychological operations (PSYOPS) may target Indian soldiers through social media misinformation, leaflets, radio broadcasts, and influence through local population contact. Recognising influence operations: information that undermines unit morale, exaggerates casualties, or encourages desertion is likely enemy PSYOPS. Counter-measures: report all suspected PSYOPS material to the intelligence officer, maintain unit cohesion and trust in the chain of command, and do not share unverified information on social media or messenger apps. Even personal photos from deployment can contribute to enemy intelligence gathering when posted online.

Understanding India's strategic geography is essential for operational soldiers. Himalayan barrier: provides natural defence against China but limits India's ability to reinforce forward areas quickly — hence massive BRO infrastructure investment. Indo-Gangetic Plain: most populous heartland containing Delhi — its defence is the strategic priority on the western front. Siliguri Corridor (Chicken's Neck): 22km wide corridor connecting Northeast India to mainland — extremely vulnerable. Andaman and Nicobar Islands: positioned 650km from the Malacca Strait — strategic chokepoint for global maritime trade. Three coastlines require strong naval power for effective defence of the peninsular.

BRO was established in 1960 following the border security lessons of the 1962 war. Mission: build and maintain roads in strategically important areas of 19 border states and union territories. Key achievements: Atal Tunnel (Rohtang) — 9km tunnel giving all-weather access to Ladakh; Darbuk-Shyok-Daulat Beg Oldie (DSDBO) road — critical Ladakh defence logistics route; Sela Tunnel connecting Arunachal Pradesh all year. BRO falls under Ministry of Defence and is staffed primarily by Corps of Engineers officers with GREF (General Reserve Engineer Force) personnel and contracted civilian labour.

Military intelligence provides information about the enemy and terrain. Intelligence cycle: Direction (commander states requirement), Collection (gathering information), Processing (converting raw data to usable intelligence), Dissemination (distributing to those who need it). Sources: HUMINT (human intelligence — informers, prisoner interrogation), SIGINT (signals intelligence — intercepting enemy communications), IMINT (imagery — satellite, UAV, aerial photographs), OSINT (open source — social media, news, public records). At unit level: patrol reports, OP logs, and captured documents are the primary intelligence sources. Intelligence is only useful if timely — a perfect report received after the enemy has moved is useless.

Captured Persons (CPERS) handling is governed by the Geneva Conventions, which India has ratified. The 5 S's of prisoner handling: Search (thorough — including hidden cavities), Silence (prevent communication between prisoners), Segregate (officers from soldiers, women from men, wounded separately), Safeguard (protect from harm including from own troops — this is a legal and moral obligation), Speed to the rear (evacuate to prisoner handling facility quickly). Prisoners must receive food, water, medical attention, and protection from violence. Abuse of prisoners is a war crime punishable under both Indian military law and international law.

Obstacles slow enemy movement and channelise them into kill zones covered by fire. Types: wire obstacles (single apron, double apron, concertina), anti-vehicle ditches (minimum 2m wide and 1.5m deep), abatis (trees felled across road), cratering (explosives destroy road surface). Key principle: every obstacle must be covered by fire — an unobserved obstacle can be breached at leisure. Anti-lifting devices: secondary fuzes placed under mines deter enemy from removing them. Wire pickets driven into ground (rather than hammered) make less noise — important for night construction in forward areas.

Ammunition management is critical — running out of ammunition in contact is a tactical failure. Basic load: INSAS — 210 rounds (7 magazines), AK-203 — 90 rounds (3 magazines). First line: carried on person. Second line: held at platoon or company level. Third line: held at battalion or brigade level. Ammunition states reported to commander: RED (less than 25% — critical), AMBER (25-50%), GREEN (above 50%). Controlled pairs: in aimed fire, shoot in pairs — reduces waste and maintains accuracy. Dud rounds: do not attempt to extract, mark location, report, await EOD. Captured enemy ammunition: use only if same calibre confirmed and checked for booby traps.

Indian Army medical evacuation follows the Role system. Role 1: Regimental Aid Post (RAP) — unit level, manned by RMO and medical assistants, stabilises casualty and prepares for evacuation. Role 2: Field Medical Centre — basic surgery and blood transfusion, holds casualties up to 48 hours. Role 3: Field Hospital — full surgical capability near the front. Role 4: Base or Command Hospital — definitive specialist treatment. Evacuation means: ambulance jeep, BMP-2 modified variant, Dhruv helicopter (high altitude), fixed-wing for long distances. Golden Hour principle: aim to get casualty to Role 2 surgical care within 1 hour of wounding.

LOAC (also called International Humanitarian Law) governs soldiers in war. Key principles: Distinction (attack only combatants and military objectives, not civilians), Proportionality (collateral damage must not be excessive relative to military advantage), Military Necessity (use only force necessary to achieve legitimate objective), Humanity (prevent unnecessary suffering). Protected persons: civilians, wounded soldiers, prisoners of war, medical personnel, chaplains. Protected symbols: Red Cross and Red Crescent on medical vehicles and personnel — attacking them is a war crime. Soldiers must understand these rules — ignorance is not a defence in a war crimes tribunal.

The Armed Forces Special Powers Act (AFSPA) applies in declared disturbed areas — currently Jammu and Kashmir and parts of Northeast India. Section 4 powers: any commissioned officer, warrant officer, JCO, or NCO can fire on persons violating law (with warning), arrest without warrant, and enter and search premises. Section 6: prosecution of soldiers requires prior Central Government sanction — protects bona fide operational acts from malicious prosecution. Limitation: AFSPA does not authorise rape, torture, or extrajudicial killing — these remain serious criminal offences. Supreme Court rulings confirm disproportionate force is NOT protected even under AFSPA.

Topic: Physical Fitness Standards for Indian Army
Content: Battle Physical Efficiency Test (BPET) for infantry: 5km run in 24 minutes (pass), 21 minutes (incentive). Beam exercises: minimum 6 chin-ups, 9-foot ditch crossing, balance beam. Annual Physical Fitness Test (APFT): push-ups minimum 20, sit-ups, 1.6km run. Special Forces selection: multi-day exercises covering 100+ km with full combat load — attrition above 90% is normal. Physical degradation at altitude: VO2 max reduces approximately 1% per 100m above sea level — a soldier at 5,000m has roughly 50% of his sea-level aerobic capacity. Maintenance standard: minimum 30 minutes aerobic exercise 5 days per week.

Carbohydrates are the primary fuel for intense activity — bread, rice, and potatoes. Proteins support muscle repair — dal, paneer, meat, eggs, and soya. Micronutrients critical for soldiers: Iron prevents anaemia and is critical for oxygen-carrying capacity at altitude (found in meat, spinach, dal). Vitamin D supports bone health and immune function (sun exposure and fortified milk). Zinc aids immune function and wound healing (meat and seeds). Pre-exercise: carbohydrate meal 2-3 hours before activity. Post-exercise: protein within 30 minutes of completion aids muscle recovery. Fasting during exercise significantly degrades performance — soldiers must eat even without appetite in cold and at altitude.

When a soldier commits a serious offence: Complaint leads to preliminary investigation by Adjutant, then Summary of Evidence (SE) recorded by an officer (witnesses examined and statements recorded), then CO decides on summary award for minor offences or Court Martial for serious offences. Types: SGCM (Summary General Court Martial — CO can convene for soldiers below officer rank, can award up to life imprisonment), DCM (District Court Martial — convened by Brigade Commander), GCM (General Court Martial — most serious, convened by Army Commander, required for officers). The accused soldier is entitled to an officer to assist his defence throughout the process.

Indian Army's digital transformation programmes. AION (Army Integrated Operations Network): classified intranet for internal communication and data sharing. ARMY-GLOBE: wide area network connecting Army establishments across India. SANGAM: tri-services secure messaging and collaboration platform. Army Portal: online services for soldiers including leave applications, document requests, and grievance submissions. SPARSH (System for Pension Administration Raksha): pensioners manage pension digitally without visiting pension offices. SAI (Secure Application for Internet): Army's approved secure messaging app built on indigenous infrastructure — approved alternative to commercial messaging apps for official communication.

The Section Commander (Havildar or Lance Naik) is the most important leadership position in the army — closest to the soldiers. Responsibilities: know every man by name and personal situation, ensure physical and mental welfare, maintain weapon and equipment readiness, lead from the front in battle, maintain discipline without illegal punishment, accurately report section combat effectiveness to platoon commander. Decision-making: must be rapid based on incomplete information — a 70% solution executed immediately beats a perfect solution that arrives too late. After action review after every exercise and operation: what happened, what went right, what must improve.

The relationship between commissioned officers, JCOs, and soldiers is the foundation of Indian Army effectiveness. JCOs (Naib Subedar, Subedar, Subedar Major) bridge the gap between commissioned officers and soldiers. They translate the officer's intent into action soldiers understand, provide institutional memory built over 15-25 years of service, and maintain discipline at the grass-roots level. A wise young officer listens to his Subedar before issuing orders — the JCO's operational experience is irreplaceable. The Subedar Major is the senior JCO in a battalion and serves as the commanding officer's trusted advisor on the pulse of the unit.

The Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) established in 1958 is India's primary defence R&D body under the Ministry of Defence. It operates 52 laboratories across India. Key achievements: Agni and Prithvi missile series, Arjun MBT, Tejas LCA, BrahMos (joint with Russia), Akash SAM, Nag ATGM, ATAGS howitzer. Technology Development Fund (TDF) funds private sector defence R&D. Atmanirbhar Bharat defence policy has significantly increased private sector participation with a positive import ban list of 300+ items to force indigenous production. DPP (Defence Procurement Procedure) gives preference to Make in India.

Route clearance finds and neutralises IEDs along a route before main body movement. Formation: lead Husky vehicle with ground-penetrating radar, followed by Buffalo armoured vehicle with robotic arm for IED extraction, security elements on flanks, EOD team, and medical cover. Visual search: soldiers search verges, culverts, and suspicious objects on foot where radar cannot reach. Controlled entry: route cleared from one end before any traffic allowed through. Rate of advance: approximately 1-2 km per hour for thorough clearance. In J&K: specialised route clearance teams exist in each sector with dedicated equipment and standing operating procedures.

Explosive Ordnance Disposal is one of the most dangerous military specialisations. First responder actions: stop, assess, mark (do not touch), cordon (minimum 300m), inform chain of command, await trained EOD personnel. IED trigger types: pressure plate (weight-activated), command wire (observer-detonated), victim-operated (tripwire or tilt switch), RCIED (radio-controlled — phone or remote detonated). Electronic countermeasures (ECM) jammers block RCIED triggers — all patrol vehicles in J&K are equipped. EOD operators use remote cameras, robotic vehicles, water-jet disruptors to neutralise devices, and controlled explosion as last resort. Never attempt personal investigation of a suspected device.

Organophosphate poisoning can occur from nerve agents (military) or agricultural pesticide exposure. Mechanism: inhibits acetylcholinesterase enzyme causing accumulation of acetylcholine. Mnemonic SLUDGE for symptoms: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis (vomiting). Additional signs: DUMBELS — Diarrhoea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation. Key sign: pinpoint pupils (miosis) with muscle twitching. Treatment: Atropine 2-4mg IV or IM immediately, repeat every 5-10 minutes until secretions dry — Atropine is the antidote. Pralidoxime reactivates cholinesterase if given within hours of exposure. Remove contaminated clothing and decontaminate skin with large volumes of water.

Regimental traditions are a powerful motivating force in the Indian Army. Izzat (honour and pride): a soldier's individual actions reflect on the entire regiment — this concept motivates maintaining standards under extreme pressure. Battle honours inscribed on regimental colours represent famous engagements the regiment fought in. Regimental centres train recruits and provide reinforcements. Soldiers refer to their regiment as paltan — a word carrying deep emotional resonance. Regimental temples, gurudwaras, and mosques are maintained at centres — faith is a key part of regimental identity that transcends the diversity of India's population.

Sainik Dharm is the ethical framework guiding the Indian soldier. Key principles: Nishkam Karma (duty without expectation of reward — Bhagavad Gita concept), protection of the innocent and weak, loyalty to the nation above personal interest, truthfulness even when inconvenient. The Chetwode Credo enshrines: the safety, honour, and welfare of your country comes first; then the honour, welfare, and comfort of your soldiers; and last, your own ease, comfort, and safety. Corruption in procurement, arms trafficking, and misuse of regimental funds violate Sainik Dharm and are serious punishable offences.

Operation Meghdoot was India's military operation to secure the Siachen Glacier before Pakistan could occupy it. Launched on April 13, 1984, Indian Army units were helilifted to key passes including Sia La and Bilafond La — arriving just hours before Pakistani troops were planned to do the same. Key units: Kumaon Regiment and Punjab Regiment were the first deployed. The operation secured the entire glacier for India — establishing the world's highest battlefield at altitudes exceeding 6,000m. India spends approximately 5-7 crore rupees per day to maintain Siachen positions. More soldiers are lost to weather and altitude than to enemy action.

Surgical strikes 2016: On September 29, Para SF units conducted cross-LOC strikes against terrorist launch pads in Pakistan-administered Kashmir following the Uri attack (September 18) that killed 18 soldiers. Multiple Para SF teams crossed the LOC simultaneously, destroyed camps, and returned. India officially announced the strikes. Balakot 2019: On February 26, Indian Air Force Mirage 2000H jets struck the Jaish-e-Mohammed training camp at Balakot inside Pakistani territory — the first IAF strike inside Pakistan since 1971. Background was the Pulwama attack (February 14) killing 40 CRPF personnel. Both operations demonstrated India's willingness to use offensive action beyond its borders in response to terrorism.

The National Defence Academy (NDA) at Khadakwasla, Pune, jointly trains officers for all three services over a 3-year (6-term) course. Entry is through the UPSC NDA exam conducted twice yearly — one of India's most competitive examinations. Graduates receive a degree from Jawaharlal Nehru University. After NDA: Army cadets proceed to IMA Dehradun (1 year), Navy cadets to INA Ezhimala (1 year), and Air Force cadets to AFA Hyderabad (1.5 years). NDA is considered the most prestigious officer entry route. The President's Cadet is the best all-round cadet of each course.

The Indian Military Academy (IMA) at Dehradun is the premier officer training institution of the Indian Army. Cadets undergo 18-month training covering academic subjects, military tactics, weapons handling, leadership development, and physical fitness. The Chetwode Hall Credo defines the officer's priorities: country first, soldiers second, self last. Passing Out Parade (POP) at the famous parade ground is attended by thousands. The Sword of Honour is awarded to the best cadet of each course. IMA also trains officers from over 28 friendly foreign nations — a source of significant strategic soft power for India.

Pay structure under the 7th Pay Commission (implemented 2016). Basic pay for Other Ranks: Sepoy Level 3 (₹21,700), Naik Level 4 (₹25,500), Havildar Level 5 (₹29,200), Naib Subedar Level 6 (₹35,400), Subedar Level 7 (₹44,900), Subedar Major Level 8 (₹47,600). Military Service Pay (MSP) of ₹5,200 per month is paid to all JCOs and Other Ranks in addition to basic pay. High Altitude Allowance reaches up to ₹25,000 per month for Zone V (Siachen area). Field Area Allowance, free rations, and free accommodation significantly increase total emoluments beyond basic pay alone.

ECHS established in 2003 provides healthcare to Ex-Servicemen and their dependents — covering over 50 lakh ESM and approximately 1 crore dependents across India. Structure: ECHS Polyclinics at station level for outpatient treatment, empanelled private hospitals for cashless specialist and surgical care, and government hospitals. One-time contribution at retirement — amount varies by rank and last pay drawn. Services covered: OPD consultations, specialist referrals, surgery, hospitalisation, and medicines. Dental treatment available at polyclinics. ECHS card must be updated when changing address or adding family dependents.

Topic: Service Documents Every Soldier Must Know
Content: Key service documents: Army Book 64 (AB-64) — the soldier's personal document containing service record, medical grading, leave account, and identity particulars — must be maintained carefully. Military ID: must be carried at all times in uniform. Nomination forms for bank account and insurance must be kept current — critical for family welfare if soldier is killed. AGI (Army Group Insurance) is collective life insurance mandatory for all soldiers with premiums deducted from pay. A registered will prevents family disputes over assets. Updated documents prevent delays in pension and other benefits flowing to the soldier's family after service or death.

Eye injuries are common in blast and fragmentation incidents. Corneal abrasion (foreign body): do not rub, irrigate with clean water, patch both eyes as they move together. Penetrating eye injury: do not remove any embedded object, cover with rigid shield (not pressure bandage), evacuate urgently — permanent vision loss results from mishandling. Flash blindness: temporary from explosions or laser — reassure the casualty, vision usually returns within minutes to hours. Chemical splash: irrigate immediately with large volumes of water for minimum 20 minutes. The golden rule for serious eye injury: cover without compression, do not remove objects, and evacuate.

Lightning strikes are a genuine hazard in mountainous postings in Ladakh, Uttarakhand, and Arunachal Pradesh during monsoon. Contrary to popular belief, a lightning strike victim carries no residual electrical charge and is completely safe to touch. CPR should be started immediately for cardiac arrest from lightning — recovery rate is high if CPR begins within minutes. Burns at entry and exit wound sites require standard burns treatment. Temporary neurological effects including confusion, memory loss, and hearing loss are common and usually resolve. Prevention: avoid exposed high ground, lone trees, open water, and metal objects during electrical storms — always descend or seek shelter.

Foot care is a combat effectiveness priority for infantry. Trench foot (non-freezing cold injury): prolonged exposure of feet to cold and wet above 0 degrees Celsius — causes swelling, redness, numbness, and pain. Prevention: change wet socks twice daily minimum, dry feet completely before putting on socks, never sleep in wet socks, use foot powder. Blisters: prevent with properly fitted boots. If blister is large and painful: sterilise needle, pierce at the edge, leave skin intact as natural dressing, apply antiseptic, and cover. Athlete's foot in hot weather: keep feet dry, change socks daily, apply Clotrimazole powder. Ill-fitting boots are a significant combat ineffectiveness issue.

Visual signals allow communication without radio in noise-restricted or radio-silent situations. Standard Indian Army hand signals: Come here (beckoning), Halt (raised fist), Enemy seen (pointing finger), Take cover (pushing hands downward), Move forward (arm swung forward), Speed up (circular arm motion), Section attack (arm pointed at objective then sweeping). VS-17 signal panel: bright orange/pink panel used for air-ground identification, ground-to-air communication, and marking positions for extraction. Heliograph mirror: sunlight reflection used for long-distance signalling — visible up to 50km in clear conditions. All soldiers must know and practice hand signals until they are instinctive.

Fire provides warmth, water purification, cooking, signalling, and psychological comfort. Fire triangle: fuel, heat, oxygen. Preparation: tinder (dry grass, cotton lint, shredded bark), kindling (pencil-thin sticks), fuel (larger logs). Methods: military lighter (most reliable — maintain as priority kit), waterproof matches, flint and steel striker (reliable in wet conditions), bow drill (friction method — effective but slow, requires dry softwood).
