Never start a fire in a hide position — smoke and light betray the position. In snow: construct a raised platform under the fire to prevent it sinking into the snow and extinguishing itself.

In a survival situation, being found is the primary task after immediate safety. Visual signals (most important — visible to aircraft during daylight): signal mirror visible up to 50km in clear weather, bright colour panels, smoke (dark smoke visible against light sky, light smoke against dark jungle). Audio: whistle — three blasts is the internationally recognised distress signal. Electronic: EPIRB or PLB (Personal Locator Beacon) sends GPS position to search and rescue satellites. Rule of threes for signals: three fires in a triangle, three whistle blasts, three shots. Start signalling as soon as the decision to attract rescue is made — do not delay.

Indian Air Force fast jet fleet: Su-30 MKI is the primary air superiority fighter — twin-engine, supermaneuverable, can carry BrahMos-A air-launched cruise missile. Mirage 2000 provides precision strike capability with laser-guided bombs and SCALP cruise missile — used in the 2019 Balakot airstrikes. Rafale (inducted 2020): Meteor BVR missile with range exceeding 100km, SCALP cruise missile, SPECTRA electronic warfare suite — currently the most capable IAF fighter. MiG-29K operates from INS Vikramaditya for fleet air defence. IAF is acquiring additional Rafales and evaluating the MQ-9B armed drone and MRFA (Multi Role Fighter Aircraft) to replace ageing MiG-21s.

Indian Navy operates both conventional and nuclear submarines. Conventional diesel-electric: Kalvari class — 6 Scorpene-type submarines of French design built at MDL Mumbai. Nuclear: INS Arihant (SSBN — carries K-15 Sagarika SLBMs, completing India's nuclear triad), INS Arighat (second SSBN), INS Chakra (nuclear attack submarine leased from Russia). Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) against enemy submarines: P-8I Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft, helicopter sonobuoys, ship-mounted sonar, and lightweight torpedoes. Future: 6 additional conventional submarines under P-75I project with air-independent propulsion (AIP) for longer underwater endurance.

INS Vikrant (IAC-1) is India's first indigenously built aircraft carrier, commissioned September 2022. Displacement 45,000 tonnes. Aircraft capacity: up to 30 aircraft including MiG-29K fighters, Ka-31 AEW helicopters, and MH-60R ASW helicopters. STOBAR configuration uses a ski-jump for aircraft takeoff and arrestor wires for recovery. Powered by 4 General Electric LM2500 gas turbines producing 88MW total — maximum speed 28 knots. Built at Cochin Shipyard. The hull steel (DMR 249A grade) was developed indigenously by DRDO and SAIL — a major achievement of the Atmanirbhar Bharat programme.

The 1971 war resulted in the creation of Bangladesh. Indian Army launched a 13-day campaign coordinated across four fronts. Key operations: capture of Dhaka, amphibious landing at Cox's Bazar, and airborne assault by 2 Para Battalion at Tangail — one of India's rare combat airborne operations. Pakistan's Eastern Command surrendered on December 16 — the largest military surrender since World War II with 93,000 POWs. Indian Navy blockaded East Pakistan and INS Vikrant prevented sea escape. General Manekshaw (later Field Marshal) commanded the campaign which is studied as a model of tri-services joint operations planning and execution.

The 1962 Sino-Indian War shaped Indian Army doctrine for decades. Key battles include the Namka Chu disaster and the heroic stand at Rezang La where Major Shaitan Singh's Charlie Company of 114 soldiers held against thousands of Chinese — all died fighting, earning Shaitan Singh a posthumous Param Vir Chakra. Lessons learned: inadequate logistics to forward posts, political interference in military decisions, poor World War II era equipment, and lack of high-altitude warfare training. Post-1962 reforms included creation of mountain divisions, significant investment in border infrastructure (now accelerated under BRO), and establishment of the Corps of Mountain Artillery.

Indian military gallantry awards in order of precedence: Param Vir Chakra (PVC) — highest wartime gallantry award, only 21 awarded since independence (all but one posthumously). Maha Vir Chakra (MVC) — second highest. Vir Chakra (VrC) — third. Ashok Chakra — highest peacetime gallantry (equivalent of PVC for non-combat gallantry). Kirti Chakra — second highest peacetime. Shaurya Chakra — third peacetime gallantry award. Sena Medal, Nau Sena Medal, Vayu Sena Medal — service-specific gallantry. Vishist Seva Medal — distinguished service. Param Vir Chakra recipients include Somnath Sharma (Poonch 1947), Abdul Hamid (1965), Arun Khetarpal (1971), Yogendra Singh Yadav (Kargil 1999).

The Indian Army maintains a regimental system with strong traditions. Major infantry regiments include: Brigade of Guards, Punjab Regiment, Grenadiers, Maratha Light Infantry, Rajput Regiment, Sikh Regiment, Sikh Light Infantry, Dogra Regiment, Garhwal Rifles, Kumaon Regiment, Assam Regiment, Bihar Regiment, Mahar Regiment, JAT Regiment, Rajputana Rifles, Naga Regiment, Ladakh Scouts. Each regiment has distinct traditions, cap badges, and geographic recruiting areas. Regimental identity builds fierce unit loyalty (izzat) which motivates soldiers in battle. Gurkha Rifles (5 regiments remaining with Indian Army) maintain Nepalese recruitment under the India-Nepal Treaty of 1947.

Indian Army doctrine published by Army Training Command (ARTRAC) is based on the concept of Ensuring Certain Victory. Key doctrinal concepts: Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs) — modular combined arms formations for faster mobilisation below division level. Network-Centric Warfare: integrating sensors, decision-makers, and weapons through digital communications. Two-Front Threat Doctrine: plans for simultaneous threat from China and Pakistan. Sub-conventional operations doctrine based on decades of experience in J&K and Northeast. Future doctrine focuses on space, cyber, and cognitive domains as new dimensions of warfare requiring non-traditional capabilities.

Key modernisation programmes: FICV (Future Infantry Combat Vehicle) — indigenous replacement for BMP-2. Arjun MK2 — upgraded MBT with Trophy active protection system. Battlefield Management System (BMS) — digital situational awareness for infantry down to section level. ATAGS (Advanced Towed Artillery Gun System) — indigenous 155mm/52 calibre howitzer with range of 48km. Loitering munitions: SkyStriker kamikaze drone procured from Israel. Directed Energy Weapons: DRDO developing DURGA (Directionally Unrestricted Ray-Gun Array) — laser weapon system. Swarm drones: under evaluation for saturation attack on air defence systems.

The Indian Army's ethos is built on several foundations: service before self as articulated in the Chetwode Credo, unity in diversity (soldiers from every religion, region, and language serving together under the regimental identity), unwavering respect for civil authority and democratic institutions (the Army has never interfered in politics — a proud tradition in South Asia), and the combination of ancient martial traditions with modern professionalism. The Indian Army has participated in over 50 United Nations peacekeeping missions — consistently among the largest contributors globally — reflecting values that extend beyond national defence. The Indian soldier is characterised by hardiness, resilience, resourcefulness, and deep loyalty to his comrades and paltan.

Field fracture management must stabilise before evacuation. Open fracture (bone visible): cover with clean dressing, do not push bone back, immobilise the limb, give antibiotics if available, and evacuate urgently — infection risk is high. Closed fracture: immobilise the joint above and below the fracture using an improvised splint made from a rifle, straight branch, or rolled sleeping mat padded with clothing. Always check circulation, sensation, and movement (CSM) distal to the fracture before and after splinting — a palpable pulse below the fracture must be confirmed. Femur fracture causes 1-2 litres of internal blood loss — always treat for haemorrhagic shock simultaneously.

Suspect spinal injury in: fall from height, vehicle rollover, blast injury, diving accident, any trauma with neck or back pain, or unconscious trauma patient. Do NOT bend, twist, or flex the spine under any circumstances. Log roll technique: minimum 3 persons — one controls the head maintaining inline spinal stabilisation, two roll the body as a unit. Improvised cervical collar: rolled sleeping mat or clothing wrapped around neck. Improvised rigid stretcher: two rifles through sleeves of two buttoned jackets. Neurological signs such as tingling, weakness, or paralysis in any limb after trauma: treat as spinal injury until proven otherwise by a doctor.

Prickly heat (Miliaria): sweat gland blockage in hot humid conditions — small red itchy bumps, treated by cooling and calamine lotion, prevented by wearing loose cotton. Tinea (ringworm, athlete's foot, jock itch): fungal infection common in humid conditions — red scaly itchy patches treated with Clotrimazole cream twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Prevention: dry skin thoroughly after washing, change socks daily. Cellulitis: bacterial skin infection — hot red swollen painful area with possible fever — requires antibiotics and elevation of the affected limb, monitor for spreading which indicates severe infection. Pressure sores in immobilised casualties: reposition every 2 hours to prevent development.

Field sanitation prevents disease — historically more soldiers die from disease than combat in prolonged campaigns. Latrine construction: minimum 30m from water source, downhill and downwind from camp, 1m deep trench, fly-proof cover, ash or lime after each use. Hand washing: mandatory after latrine use and before eating — soap and water for minimum 20 seconds. Food hygiene: all cooking utensils washed in hot soapy water then rinsed in clean water. Food covered against flies at all times. Dead animal disposal: bury immediately minimum 1m deep with lime if available. Fly control is critical — flies transmit typhoid, dysentery, and cholera between camp areas.

Combat load for an Indian infantryman: Fighting order (minimum for combat) includes weapon, ammunition (210 rounds INSAS or 90 rounds AK-203 basic load), 2 litres of water minimum, personal medical kit, 24-hour food, and radio for the section signaller. Total weight: 15-20 kg. Marching order (sustained operations) adds sleeping system, change of clothes, additional rations, full medical kit, and shelter components. Total: 35-45 kg. High altitude additions of down sleeping bag, extra clothing layers, and snow equipment add 10-15 kg more. Load management is a commander's responsibility — an overloaded soldier cannot fight or move effectively and will become a casualty from exhaustion.

Indian Army uses tiered body armour. Soft armour (NIJ Level IIIA): protects against pistol rounds and fragmentation — flexible, worn under uniform or as lightweight vest, provides no protection against rifle fire. Hard armour (NIJ Level III and IV ceramic plates): front and back plates in plate carrier — Level III stops 7.62mm rifle rounds, Level IV stops armour-piercing rounds. Ballistic helmet: Kevlar or composite material — protects against fragmentation and pistol rounds but not rifle rounds. Groin protector and neck collar available for high-threat operations. Weight trade-off: full body armour weighs 12-15 kg and significantly degrades mobility — commanders must balance protection against operational requirements.

Japanese Encephalitis (JE) is a viral brain infection transmitted by the Culex mosquito (which breeds in rice paddies and irrigation channels). Endemic in Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Assam, and Northeast states — directly relevant for soldiers posted in these areas. Symptoms: sudden fever, severe headache, stiff neck, seizures, and rapidly progressing altered consciousness. No specific antiviral treatment — only supportive care. Fatality rate 20-30%; survivors frequently suffer permanent neurological disability. Prevention: JE vaccination is mandatory for troops posted in endemic areas, combined with standard mosquito protection measures including DEET, treated uniforms, and mosquito nets.

Kala-Azar is a parasitic disease transmitted by the sandfly, endemic in Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal, and eastern UP — areas where Indian troops are stationed. Caused by the Leishmania donovani parasite. Symptoms: prolonged irregular fever lasting weeks to months, progressive weight loss, enlarged spleen and liver causing protuberant abdomen, darkening of skin (kala-azar means black fever in Hindi), and severe anaemia. If untreated it is almost universally fatal. Treatment requires Liposomal Amphotericin B administered in a hospital setting. Prevention: sandflies bite at night and rest in wall cracks — seal cracks, use insecticide spray, and use fine-mesh nets (sandflies are smaller than mosquitoes).

ROE in counter-insurgency operations under AFSPA is more permissive than peacetime but strictly governed. Right of Private Defence (Section 96-106 IPC): every citizen including a soldier has the right to defend themselves and others from harm. Warning before firing (where tactically feasible): 'Ruko, Haath Upar Karo' (Stop, Hands Up) is the standard warning in Hindi, then a warning shot if safe, then aimed fire as last resort. No firing into crowds without specific identified threat. Collateral damage: civilian casualties must be immediately reported to chain of command and investigated. ROE cards are carried by all soldiers in J&K — they define exact permissions and restrictions for each specific operational area.
